title: Strings, Byte Buffers, Length, Capacity, and Encoding · Pointer Forensics
summary: An incident responder inherits a corrupted length-aware text and byte boundary, the earliest failing input, and two plausible hypotheses; they must preserve first-failure evidence, repair the cause, and add a regression. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in length-aware text and byte boundary and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish C strings, unterminated character arrays, byte buffers, string literals, and encoded text.
Track logical length, allocated capacity, required terminator space, and maximum scan distance separately.
Use size-aware copying and formatting with return-value checks and truncation policy.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug String Buffer Distinction

C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Recognize unbounded and ambiguous string APIs as defect-analysis material and migrate them behind tested contracts.

For Publish the incident record · Debug Legacy Api Defense, challenge the documented misconception with changed-size, failure-injection, printed invariant, compiler, sanitizer, analyzer, fuzz, ABI, concurrency, or production-transfer evidence: A larger destination makes an unbounded legacy copy safe enough.
